intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

body.intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

draft.title: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments
draft.summary: Drafting your prototype from the swipes…
draft.html:
a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

Building your first draft…

data.intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

facade.hypothesis: Users prefer a relaxed, progress-oriented tone with friendly micro-interactions over clinical precision language
facade.label: Casual checkmarks
facade.acceptImplies: Build around encouraging, human tone. Use celebratory language for logged experiments. Favor conversational copy over medical terminology.
facade.rejectImplies: Shift toward more formal, data-centric voice. Emphasize scientific rigor and neutral tone. Use clinical language and minimal animation.

evidence.hypothesis: Users prefer a relaxed, progress-oriented tone with friendly micro-interactions over clinical precision language
evidence.implication: Build around encouraging, human tone. Use celebratory language for logged experiments. Favor conversational copy over medical terminology.

facade.hypothesis: Users prefer persistent navigation over collapsible menus when tracking multiple experiments
facade.axisTargeted: Navigation containment and screen real estate tradeoff
facade.label: Sidebar always visible
facade.acceptImplies: User values quick navigation between experiments and consistent orientation; likely wants dense info architecture with persistent context
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers maximized content area and minimal chrome; may favor tab-based or hamburger navigation to reclaim horizontal space

facade.hypothesis: A health dashboard works better with gentle, approachable UI elements rather than sharp or clinical geometry
facade.label: Soft rounded cards
facade.acceptImplies: User gravitates toward approachable, friendly aesthetic for health tracking—curved corners, gentle shadows, less 'medical device' feel
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers cleaner, more structured look—sharper corners, minimal curves, possibly more clinical or minimalist aesthetic

facade.hypothesis: Users prefer subtle, ambient visual feedback over stark data presentation when reviewing long-term health trends
facade.label: Warm glow
facade.acceptImplies: Dashboard benefits from softer color palettes, gradual animations, and encouraging language around experimental progress
facade.rejectImplies: Users want crisp, clinical presentation with high contrast and neutral tones to signal scientific rigor and data clarity

facade.hypothesis: Users will prefer gentle, diffused lighting over stark contrast for a health-tracking interface that feels safe and non-clinical
facade.label: Soft shadows
facade.acceptImplies: The dashboard should use warm grays, subtle gradients, and diffused light effects to create comfort and approachability—moving away from harsh blacks and bright whites that feel medical or sterile
facade.rejectImplies: The user may prefer either crisper visual contrast for clarity and efficiency, or even warmer ambient qualities that feel more organic and less designed—suggesting the softness itself isn't the right direction

facade.hypothesis: Users prefer gradual animated transitions over instant state changes when logging health data
facade.label: Smooth slide-in
facade.acceptImplies: Dashboard benefits from choreographed motion: data cards, chart reveals, and input fields sliding in rather than popping. Creates sense of flow and care.
facade.rejectImplies: Users want immediate visual feedback and instant layout—no animation overhead. Dashboard should feel snappy and responsive, not fluid.

axes.label: Whether user prefers minimal clinical aesthetics vs. warm, human-centered visual design for health data
Whether user wants data-dense dashboard layout vs. progressive disclosure with one metric at a time
axes.poleA: All at once or step by step?

axes.label: Whether user responds to motivational/encouraging tone vs. neutral, factual reporting voice
Whether user wants celebratory, gamified feedback vs. calm, meditative tracking environment
Whether user prefers raw numbers and charts vs. narrative summaries and insights
Whether user wants instant feedback on actions vs. slow, contemplative animations and transitions

facade.hypothesis: Users want celebratory, supportive language in feedback and progress summaries rather than clinical or data-heavy phrasing
facade.axisTargeted: voice and personality — warm/supportive vs neutral/clinical
facade.label: Encouraging tone, minimal jargon
facade.acceptImplies: Lean into celebratory micro-copy, use 'you're doing great' language, replace medical terms with conversational alternatives, make logging feel like a win
facade.rejectImplies: Maintain clinical precision, use technical terminology, keep copy neutral and data-focused, treat dashboard as a science tool not a companion

draft.title: Health Experiment Tracker
draft.summary: A personal dashboard for monitoring long-term health experiments with experiment cards, metrics, and progress visualization.
draft.html:
Health Experiments

Track your long-term health initiatives

Active Experiments

Sleep Optimization

Active

Testing consistent 10 PM bedtime with blue light reduction

Progress 65% (13 of 20 days)
Avg Sleep Duration 7.2 hrs
Sleep Quality 7.8/10

Morning Hydration

Active

Drinking 500ml water within 30 minutes of waking

Progress 42% (8 of 19 days)
Consistency 42%
Days Completed 8
Completed Experiments

30-Day Meditation

Completed

Daily 10-minute meditation practice

Progress 100% (30 of 30 days)
